module.blockchain.title: Blockchain
module.blockchain.description: Learn blocks, transactions, consensus, decentralization and digital ownership.

blockchain.meta.title: Blockchain Fundamentals — Nodra Academy
blockchain.hero.title: Blockchain Fundamentals
blockchain.hero.description: A complete introduction to how blockchains work: distributed ledgers, blocks, hashing, consensus mechanisms, network layers, wallets, and the security model that holds it all together.
blockchain.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
blockchain.hero.stat2.label: Duration
blockchain.hero.stat3.label: Format
blockchain.hero.stat4.label: Sections
blockchain.toc.title: Contents
blockchain.introduction.title: Introduction
blockchain.introduction.p1: A blockchain is a distributed ledger: a database that is copied and synchronized across many independent computers, called nodes, instead of being stored on a single server controlled by one entity. Every participant holds a copy of the same history, and that history is agreed upon collectively rather than dictated by a central authority.
blockchain.introduction.p2: The term "blockchain" describes the structure of that ledger: data is grouped into blocks, and each block is cryptographically linked to the one before it, forming a chain. Once a block is added and confirmed by the network, altering it would require rewriting every block that came after it on every copy of the ledger at the same time — which is what makes the record practically immutable.
blockchain.introduction.p3: This lesson walks through the core mechanics behind that idea: how blocks are built, how nodes agree on which version of the ledger is correct, how the network is organized into layers, and how users interact with it safely through wallets.
blockchain.howItWorks.title: How Blockchain Works
blockchain.howItWorks.intro: At a high level, a blockchain network performs four repeating actions: transactions are broadcast, nodes validate them, valid transactions are grouped into a block, and the network agrees on adding that block to the chain.
blockchain.howItWorks.card1.title: Decentralization
blockchain.howItWorks.card1.text: No single computer owns the ledger. Thousands of independent nodes each keep a full or partial copy, so there is no single point of failure or control.
blockchain.howItWorks.card2.title: Transactions
blockchain.howItWorks.card2.text: A transaction is a signed instruction, such as "send 0.5 units from address A to address B." It is broadcast to the network and waits to be validated.
blockchain.howItWorks.card3.title: Validation
blockchain.howItWorks.card3.text: Nodes check that a transaction follows the protocol's rules — correct signature, sufficient balance, no double-spending — before including it in a block.
blockchain.howItWorks.card4.title: Immutability
blockchain.howItWorks.card4.text: Once a block is confirmed and buried under later blocks, changing it becomes computationally and economically impractical.
blockchain.anatomy.title: Anatomy of a Block
blockchain.anatomy.intro: Every block is made of a header and a body. The header contains metadata used to link and verify the block; the body contains the actual transaction data.
blockchain.anatomy.field1.label: Block Number
blockchain.anatomy.field2.label: Timestamp
blockchain.anatomy.field3.label: Previous Hash
blockchain.anatomy.field4.label: Merkle Root
blockchain.anatomy.field5.label: Nonce
blockchain.anatomy.field6.label: Block Hash
blockchain.anatomy.field7.label: Transactions
blockchain.anatomy.li1: Previous Hash — links this block to the one before it, forming the chain.
blockchain.anatomy.li2: Merkle Root — a single hash that summarizes every transaction in the block, allowing quick verification without re-checking each one individually.
blockchain.anatomy.li3: Nonce — a number miners adjust repeatedly while searching for a valid block hash under Proof of Work.
blockchain.anatomy.li4: Block Hash — the fingerprint of the entire block header, recalculated by every node to confirm the block is valid.
blockchain.hashDemo.title: Hash Demo
blockchain.hashDemo.intro: A cryptographic hash function takes any input and returns a fixed-length string of characters. The same input always produces the same output, but even a one-character change produces a completely different result. This is what makes tampering with a block detectable.
blockchain.hashDemo.playgroundLabel: Try it with your own input — hashed live with SHA-256
blockchain.hashDemo.note: Notice the second input only changes the capitalization of one letter, yet the resulting hash is entirely different from the first — this is called the avalanche effect. Every hash on this page is computed live in your browser using SHA-256; nothing is sent to a server.
blockchain.consensus.title: Consensus
blockchain.consensus.intro: Because there is no central authority, the network needs a way for independent nodes to agree on a single, shared version of the ledger. The two most widely used models are Proof of Work and Proof of Stake.
blockchain.consensus.pow.title: Proof of Work
blockchain.consensus.pow.text: Miners compete to solve a computationally expensive puzzle: finding a nonce that produces a hash below a target value. Solving it is hard; checking the solution is easy.
blockchain.consensus.pow.li2: Security comes from the cost of computing power.
blockchain.consensus.pow.li3: High energy consumption is its main criticism.
blockchain.consensus.pos.title: Proof of Stake
blockchain.consensus.pos.text: Validators lock up ("stake") an amount of the network's native asset. The protocol selects a validator to propose the next block, weighted by stake size.
blockchain.consensus.pos.li1: Used by Ethereum, Cardano, and Solana.
blockchain.consensus.pos.li2: Security comes from the economic value at stake.
blockchain.consensus.pos.li3: Dramatically lower energy consumption than PoW.
blockchain.layers.title: Layer 1 vs Layer 2
blockchain.layers.intro: As blockchains grew in usage, a single base network struggled to process every transaction quickly and cheaply. This led to a layered architecture.
blockchain.layers.l1.title: Layer 1
blockchain.layers.l1.text: The base blockchain itself — Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana. It handles consensus, security, and final settlement directly.
blockchain.layers.l2.title: Layer 2
blockchain.layers.l2.text: A protocol built on top of a Layer 1 that processes transactions off-chain and later settles a summary back onto it, increasing throughput and lowering fees.
blockchain.wallets.title: Wallets
blockchain.wallets.intro: A wallet does not "store" coins the way a physical wallet stores cash. It stores the private keys that prove ownership of assets recorded on the blockchain.
blockchain.wallets.hot.title: Hot Wallet
blockchain.wallets.hot.text: Connected to the internet at all times — mobile apps, browser extensions, exchange accounts. Convenient, but more exposed to remote attacks.
blockchain.wallets.cold.title: Cold Wallet
blockchain.wallets.cold.text: Keeps private keys offline — hardware devices or paper backups. Far more resistant to remote hacking, at the cost of everyday convenience.
blockchain.wallets.closing: A common practice is to keep small, frequently used amounts in a hot wallet and larger holdings in a cold wallet.
blockchain.security.title: Security
blockchain.security.intro: Blockchain security is a mix of cryptography, economic incentives, and network design.
blockchain.security.li1: Private keys are the real target. Most losses come from stolen or leaked keys, not from breaking the blockchain's cryptography.
blockchain.security.li2: 51% attacks. If a single actor controls the majority of a network's mining power or stake, they could theoretically rewrite recent history.
blockchain.security.li3: Smart contract risk. Code deployed on-chain can contain bugs; audits reduce but do not eliminate this risk.
blockchain.security.li4: Phishing and social engineering. The most common attack vector targets the user directly, not the protocol.
blockchain.firstSteps.title: First Steps
blockchain.firstSteps.intro: A practical path for putting this lesson into practice, from observation to a safe first interaction.
blockchain.firstSteps.step1Text: Open a public block explorer for any major chain and inspect a real block: its hash, its transactions, its timestamp.
blockchain.firstSteps.step2Title: Create a wallet on a testnet
blockchain.firstSteps.step2Text: Install a wallet and switch it to a test network, where assets have no real value, to practice safely.
blockchain.firstSteps.step3Text: Request free test tokens from a faucet and send a transaction to see the full lifecycle in action.
blockchain.firstSteps.step4Text: Move on to the next lesson in the Academy to go deeper into wallets and self-custody.
blockchain.glossary.title: Glossary
blockchain.glossary.def1: A computer participating in the network, holding a copy of the ledger and enforcing its rules.
blockchain.glossary.def2: A fixed-length output produced by feeding data into a cryptographic hash function.
blockchain.glossary.def3: A number that miners vary to find a valid block hash under Proof of Work.
blockchain.glossary.def4: A single hash summarizing all transactions in a block, used for quick verification.
blockchain.glossary.def5: A network participant responsible for proposing and confirming new blocks.
blockchain.glossary.def6: A secret value that proves ownership of an address and is used to sign transactions.
blockchain.glossary.def7: A Layer 2 solution that executes transactions off-chain and posts compressed results back to Layer 1.
blockchain.glossary.def8: Attempting to spend the same digital asset more than once, prevented by network validation.
blockchain.quiz.title: Quiz
blockchain.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on.
blockchain.quiz.q1: 1. What links one block to the next in a blockchain?
blockchain.quiz.q1o2: B. The previous block's hash stored in the new block's header
blockchain.quiz.q1o3: C. A central server assigning order
blockchain.quiz.q1o4: D. The wallet address of the miner
blockchain.quiz.q2: 2. In Proof of Work, what makes the system secure?
blockchain.quiz.q2o1: A. The identity of the miners
blockchain.quiz.q2o2: B. The cost of the computing power required to redo the work
blockchain.quiz.q2o4: D. The size of the Merkle root
blockchain.quiz.q3: 3. What is the main advantage of a Layer 2 network?
blockchain.quiz.q3o1: A. It replaces the Layer 1 entirely
blockchain.quiz.q3o2: B. It increases throughput and lowers fees while inheriting Layer 1 security
blockchain.quiz.q3o3: C. It removes the need for consensus
blockchain.quiz.q3o4: D. It stores private keys for users
blockchain.quiz.q4: 4. Why is a cold wallet generally considered safer for long-term storage?
blockchain.quiz.q4o1: A. It has a stronger internet connection
blockchain.quiz.q4o2: B. It keeps private keys offline, away from remote attacks
blockchain.quiz.q4o3: C. It is faster for daily transactions
blockchain.quiz.q4o4: D. It is managed by an exchange
blockchain.quiz.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review the section above and try again.
wallets.meta.title: Wallets & Self Custody — Nodra Academy
wallets.hero.title: Wallets & Self Custody
wallets.hero.description: What a wallet actually stores, how public and private keys work together, what a seed phrase really protects, and how to send and receive assets without losing them to a careless mistake.
wallets.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
wallets.hero.stat2.label: Duration
wallets.hero.stat3.label: Format
wallets.hero.stat4.label: Sections
wallets.toc.title: Contents
wallets.introduction.title: Introduction
wallets.introduction.p1: The word "wallet" is misleading. A physical wallet holds cash — if you hand it over, the cash is gone. A crypto wallet holds none of your assets at all. Every coin, token, and NFT you own already lives on the blockchain, permanently, in a public ledger that anyone can inspect. What your wallet actually holds is something closer to a keychain: the cryptographic keys that prove those assets are yours and let you authorize moving them.
wallets.introduction.p2: This distinction matters more than it sounds. If you understand that a wallet is a key manager, not a vault of coins, almost everything else in this lesson — seed phrases, custodial services, hardware devices — becomes a natural consequence of one simple question: who controls the keys?
wallets.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you will know exactly what lives inside a wallet, how to tell a safe setup from a risky one, and how to send and receive assets without making the mistakes that cost beginners the most money.
wallets.anatomy.title: What's Really Inside a Wallet
wallets.anatomy.p1: Think of your blockchain balance as an entry in a giant public spreadsheet that every node in the network keeps a copy of. Your wallet doesn't hold the money in that spreadsheet — it holds the credentials that let you edit the row that belongs to you. Opening a wallet app is a bit like opening a key management app: three pieces of data make the whole system work.
wallets.anatomy.field1.label: Public Address
wallets.anatomy.field2.label: Public Key
wallets.anatomy.field3.label: Private Key
wallets.anatomy.field4.label: Derivation Path
wallets.anatomy.field5.label: Balance (on-chain)
wallets.anatomy.p2: Notice the balance is the only thing here that actually lives on the blockchain — it is read from the network, not stored locally. Everything above it is what your wallet generates and safeguards. The private key is the only truly secret piece; everything derived from it can be shown, shared, or rebuilt.
wallets.keys.title: Public Key vs Private Key
wallets.keys.intro: A useful analogy: your public address is like a mailbox with your name on it, bolted to the street where anyone can see it and drop letters in. Your private key is the only key that opens that mailbox and lets you take what's inside — or send something out signed with your name. You can publish the mailbox address everywhere without any risk; the moment someone else holds the key, the mailbox is no longer yours.
wallets.keys.public.title: Public Key / Address
wallets.keys.public.text: Derived mathematically from your private key, but impossible to reverse. Safe to share with anyone — it's how people send you assets, and how the network verifies your signatures.
wallets.keys.public.li1: Safe to post publicly, print, or read aloud.
wallets.keys.public.li2: Used to receive funds and verify signed transactions.
wallets.keys.public.li3: Sharing it can never move your funds.
wallets.keys.private.title: Private Key
wallets.keys.private.text: A secret number that mathematically proves ownership. Whoever holds it can sign transactions and move the funds — no password reset, no customer support, no exceptions.
wallets.keys.private.li1: Must never be typed into a website or shared with anyone.
wallets.keys.private.li2: Used to sign transactions, proving they came from you.
wallets.keys.private.li3: If it leaks, your funds can be moved instantly and irreversibly.
wallets.keys.closing: Every legitimate wallet, exchange, or support agent will only ever ask for your public address. The single most reliable way to spot a scam is a request for your private key or seed phrase — genuine services never need it.
wallets.seedPhrase.title: Seed Phrase
wallets.seedPhrase.p1: Modern wallets don't generate one private key — they can generate thousands, one for every asset and account inside the wallet. Memorizing all of them would be impossible, so wallets condense the master secret that produces every single key into one human-readable backup: a sequence of 12 or 24 common words, in a specific order, called a seed phrase (or recovery phrase).
wallets.seedPhrase.p2: Anyone who has your seed phrase can regenerate every private key your wallet ever created, on any device, anywhere in the world — with no PIN and no permission needed. It is not "a backup among others"; it is the single master key to everything. Treat the words below the way you'd treat the title deed to a house.
wallets.seedPhrase.p3: This demo is a harmless example — but notice how it feels to have it blurred by default. That's the correct default for a real seed phrase, always: hidden, offline, and never photographed, typed into a website, or stored in a notes app, email, or cloud drive.
wallets.seedPhrase.li1: Write it on paper (or metal). A physical, offline copy can't be phished, hacked, or leaked through a data breach.
wallets.seedPhrase.li2: Never enter it into a website. No legitimate wallet or exchange will ever ask you to type your seed phrase into a browser form.
wallets.seedPhrase.li3: Store copies in separate secure locations. This protects against fire, theft, or loss of a single copy.
wallets.seedPhrase.li4: If it's ever exposed, move your funds immediately. Create a brand-new wallet and transfer everything — an exposed seed phrase cannot be "reset."
wallets.custodial.title: Custodial vs Non-Custodial
wallets.custodial.intro: The single most important question about any wallet is: who actually holds the private keys? The answer splits every wallet in existence into one of two categories.
wallets.custodial.custodial.title: Custodial Wallet
wallets.custodial.custodial.text: A third party — typically an exchange — holds the private keys on your behalf. You log in with a username and password, like a bank account. This is convenient and forgiving of mistakes (support can help with a lost password), but it means you don't truly own the assets: you own a claim against the company.
wallets.custodial.custodial.li1: "Not your keys, not your coins" — a common saying in the space.
wallets.custodial.custodial.li2: If the company is hacked, goes bankrupt, or freezes withdrawals, you can lose access.
wallets.custodial.custodial.li3: Good fit for beginners moving small amounts or trading actively.
wallets.custodial.nonCustodial.title: Non-Custodial Wallet
wallets.custodial.nonCustodial.text: You alone generate and hold the private keys — typically as a seed phrase only you know. Nobody can freeze, seize, or lose your funds on your behalf, but nobody can help you recover them either. Full control comes with full responsibility.
wallets.custodial.nonCustodial.li1: True ownership — the assets are yours as long as the keys are safe.
wallets.custodial.nonCustodial.li2: No customer support can reset a lost seed phrase.
wallets.custodial.nonCustodial.li3: Better fit for long-term holdings and larger amounts.
wallets.hotCold.title: Hot Wallet vs Cold Wallet
wallets.hotCold.intro: This is a separate axis from custodial vs non-custodial: it's about whether your private keys ever touch a device connected to the internet.
wallets.hotCold.hot.title: Hot Wallet
wallets.hotCold.hot.text: A wallet app on a phone, browser extension, or desktop program — connected to the internet at all times. Signing a transaction takes seconds, which makes it ideal for everyday spending, but the private key sits on a device that malware, phishing pages, or a compromised browser could reach.
wallets.hotCold.cold.title: Cold Wallet
wallets.hotCold.cold.text: Private keys are generated and stored on a device that is never connected to the internet — a hardware wallet or even a sheet of paper. Transactions are signed offline and only the signed result touches the internet, which makes remote theft dramatically harder.
wallets.hotCold.closing: A practical rule many experienced users follow: keep a small "spending" amount in a hot wallet, the way you'd carry cash in a pocket, and keep the bulk of your holdings in cold storage, the way you'd use a safe.
wallets.hardware.title: Hardware Wallets
wallets.hardware.p1: A hardware wallet is a small dedicated device — resembling a USB drive — built for a single purpose: generating and storing private keys in a chip that never exposes them, even to the computer it's plugged into. When you approve a transaction, the unsigned data is sent to the device, signed internally, and only the signature is sent back. The private key itself never leaves the chip, not even during use.
wallets.hardware.p2: This is what makes hardware wallets the standard recommendation for holding meaningful amounts long-term: even a computer that is fully infected with malware cannot extract the key, because the key is never present in the computer's memory in the first place.
wallets.hardware.card1.title: Isolated Signing
wallets.hardware.card1.text: Transactions are signed inside the device's secure chip; the private key never touches the connected computer or phone.
wallets.hardware.card2.title: Physical Confirmation
wallets.hardware.card2.text: You approve every transaction with a physical button press on the device itself, so malware on your computer can't silently approve one for you.
wallets.sendingReceiving.title: Sending & Receiving Assets
wallets.sendingReceiving.intro: Every outgoing transaction follows the same four-step path, whether you're using a hot wallet on your phone or a hardware wallet on your desk.
wallets.sendingReceiving.li1: User — you decide to send an amount to a specific address and initiate the request in your wallet app.
wallets.sendingReceiving.li2: Wallet — the app builds the transaction: sender, recipient, amount, and network fee.
wallets.sendingReceiving.li3: Signature — your private key signs the transaction, mathematically proving it came from you, without ever being revealed.
wallets.sendingReceiving.li4: Blockchain — the signed transaction is broadcast, validated by nodes, and permanently recorded in a block.
wallets.sendingReceiving.closing: Receiving works in reverse and requires nothing from you but sharing your public address — often as a scannable QR code — for the sender's wallet to fill in automatically, removing any chance of a typing mistake.
wallets.security.title: Security Best Practices
wallets.security.intro: Most losses in this space come from a handful of preventable habits, not from exotic hacks. These practices remove almost all of the realistic risk.
wallets.security.li1: Verify the full recipient address. Check the first and last several characters at minimum — malware is known to swap addresses in your clipboard.
wallets.security.li2: Send a small test amount first. For any large or first-time transfer, send a tiny amount, confirm it arrived, then send the rest.
wallets.security.li3: Use a hardware wallet for significant holdings. Keep only spending money in hot wallets connected to the internet.
wallets.security.li4: Bookmark official sites; never search for them. Search ads and lookalike domains are a leading source of phishing.
wallets.security.li5: Enable all available authentication layers on custodial accounts — a strong password plus two-factor authentication, never SMS alone if an authenticator app is available.
wallets.mistakes.title: Common Mistakes
wallets.mistakes.intro: Nearly every avoidable loss traces back to one of these five mistakes.
wallets.mistakes.li1: Typing a seed phrase into a website. No legitimate service ever needs it — this is always a phishing attempt, no matter how official the page looks.
wallets.mistakes.li2: Sending to the wrong network. The same address can exist on multiple chains; sending an asset on the wrong network can make it unrecoverable.
wallets.mistakes.li3: Storing a seed phrase digitally. Photos, notes apps, and cloud backups are all searchable by malware and cloud account breaches.
wallets.mistakes.li4: Skipping the test transaction. A single mistyped character in an address, sent at full amount, is one of the most common and painful losses.
wallets.mistakes.li5: Trusting unsolicited "support". Real support never DMs you first, never asks for your seed phrase, and never asks you to share your screen during a "fix."
wallets.glossary.title: Glossary
wallets.glossary.def1: Software or hardware that generates, stores, and uses cryptographic keys to interact with a blockchain — it does not store the assets themselves.
wallets.glossary.def2: A shareable identifier derived from a private key, used to receive assets and verify signatures.
wallets.glossary.def3: A secret number that proves ownership of an address and is used to sign outgoing transactions.
wallets.glossary.def4: A 12–24 word backup that can regenerate every private key a wallet has ever created.
wallets.glossary.def5: A wallet where a third party holds the private keys on the user's behalf, such as an exchange account.
wallets.glossary.def6: A wallet where the user alone holds and controls the private keys.
wallets.glossary.def7: A wallet whose keys are generated or stored on a device connected to the internet.
wallets.glossary.def8: A wallet whose keys are generated and stored fully offline, such as a hardware wallet.
wallets.glossary.def9: A dedicated physical device that signs transactions internally without ever exposing the private key.
wallets.glossary.def10: The standardized formula a wallet uses to generate many key pairs from a single seed phrase.
wallets.quiz.title: Quiz
wallets.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
wallets.quiz.q1: 1. What does a wallet actually store?
wallets.quiz.q1o1: A. The coins and tokens themselves
wallets.quiz.q1o2: B. The cryptographic keys that prove ownership of on-chain assets
wallets.quiz.q1o3: C. A copy of the entire blockchain
wallets.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review What's Inside a Wallet and try again.
wallets.quiz.q2: 2. What is safe to share publicly with someone who wants to send you funds?
wallets.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Public Key vs Private Key and try again.
wallets.quiz.q3: 3. What is a seed phrase used for?
wallets.quiz.q3o1: A. Logging into an exchange account
wallets.quiz.q3o2: B. Regenerating every private key the wallet has created
wallets.quiz.q3o4: D. Verifying your identity with the government
wallets.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Seed Phrase and try again.
wallets.quiz.q4: 4. In a custodial wallet, who controls the private keys?
wallets.quiz.q4o2: B. The third-party service, such as an exchange
wallets.quiz.q4o3: C. A hardware device you own
wallets.quiz.q4o4: D. No one — custodial wallets have no keys
wallets.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Custodial vs Non-Custodial and try again.
wallets.quiz.q5: 5. Which option is generally safest for storing large amounts long-term?
wallets.quiz.q5o1: A. A hot wallet on your phone
wallets.quiz.q5o3: C. A hardware wallet, kept offline
wallets.quiz.q5o4: D. An exchange account with no 2FA
wallets.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Hardware Wallets and try again.
wallets.quiz.q6: 6. What is the single best habit to avoid the most common, costly mistake when sending funds?
wallets.quiz.q6o1: A. Sending the full amount immediately to save time
wallets.quiz.q6o2: B. Sending a small test amount first and confirming it arrived
wallets.quiz.q6o3: C. Sharing your seed phrase with the recipient
wallets.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Common Mistakes and try again.
networks.meta.title: Blockchain Networks — Nodra Academy
networks.hero.title: Blockchain Networks
networks.hero.description: What actually makes a blockchain network, why so many of them exist, how transactions get validated, what gas fees pay for, and how Layer 1s, Layer 2s, and bridges fit together.
networks.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
networks.hero.stat2.label: Duration
networks.hero.stat3.label: Format
networks.hero.stat4.label: Sections
networks.toc.title: Contents
networks.introduction.title: Introduction
networks.introduction.p1: A blockchain does not exist as a single global thing — it exists as many separate networks, each running its own independent ledger, its own rules, and its own set of validators. Bitcoin, Ethereum, and dozens of others are not different "brands" of the same network; they are, structurally, completely separate systems that happen to share the same underlying idea.
networks.introduction.p2: This lesson looks at what actually makes a "network" in this context: how transactions are validated, why fees exist, how networks scale through layers, and how assets sometimes move between one network and another. None of this requires guessing at any asset's price or making an investment decision — it is infrastructure, and infrastructure is worth understanding on its own terms.
networks.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you will be able to explain, in plain terms, what a blockchain network is, why so many of them exist, how a transaction gets validated, what gas fees pay for, the difference between a Layer 1 and a Layer 2, and how to avoid the handful of mistakes that most often cost beginners money when moving between networks.
networks.whatIsNetwork.title: What Is a Blockchain Network
networks.whatIsNetwork.p1: Strip away the branding and a blockchain network is three things working together: a set of computers (nodes) that each keep a full copy of the same ledger, a set of rules (a protocol) that every node agrees to follow, and a mechanism (consensus) that lets thousands of independent nodes agree on which transactions are valid and in what order — without any single party being in charge.
networks.whatIsNetwork.field1.label: Network Name
networks.whatIsNetwork.field2.label: Native Token
networks.whatIsNetwork.field3.label: Consensus Mechanism
networks.whatIsNetwork.field4.label: Average Block Time
networks.whatIsNetwork.field5.label: Status
networks.whatIsNetwork.p2: Every network you'll ever interact with — no matter its name — is some specific combination of these same three ingredients: who can run a node, what rule set they enforce, and how they reach agreement.
networks.whyDifferentChains.title: Why Different Blockchains Exist
networks.whyDifferentChains.intro: If one network could do everything perfectly, there would be no reason for more than one to exist. In practice, every design choice involves a trade-off, and different networks make different trade-offs on purpose.
networks.whyDifferentChains.general.text: Built to run any kind of application through smart contracts — from simple transfers to complex programs. They prioritize flexibility and a broad ecosystem of tools over any single use case.
Optimized for one job — for example, moving payments quickly and cheaply, or supporting a single application at high speed. They trade some flexibility for performance in that specific area.
networks.whyDifferentChains.enterprise.t: Restrict who can run a node, often used by organizations that want the shared-ledger model without a fully public, anonymous set of participants.
networks.whyDifferentChains.closing: None of these designs is universally "better" — they are answers to different questions. A network optimized for maximum decentralization will rarely also be the fastest one, and a network optimized for speed will usually concentrate validation among fewer participants.
networks.howNetworkWorks.title: How a Network Works
networks.howNetworkWorks.intro: Once you understand the three ingredients above, the day-to-day operation of a network is a short, repeating cycle.
networks.howNetworkWorks.li1: Transaction Created — a user builds a request (e.g. "send X amount to this address") and signs it with their private key.
networks.howNetworkWorks.li2: Broadcast to Nodes — the signed transaction is sent out and quickly reaches many nodes across the network.
networks.howNetworkWorks.li3: Included in a Block — a node responsible for producing the next block picks up pending transactions and groups them together.
networks.howNetworkWorks.li4: Block Validated — other nodes independently check that every transaction in the block follows the rules (valid signature, sufficient balance, no duplicate spending).
networks.howNetworkWorks.li5: Added to the Chain — once enough nodes agree the block is valid, it is appended to the ledger, and every node updates its own copy.
networks.howNetworkWorks.closing: This cycle repeats continuously, and the time it takes to complete varies enormously between networks — from a couple of seconds to several minutes.
networks.validation.title: Transaction Validation
networks.validation.intro: "Validation" is the step that turns a raw request into something the entire network trusts. Two things need to be true before a transaction is accepted.
networks.validation.correctness.title: Correctness
networks.validation.correctness.text: The transaction must be properly signed by the sender's private key, follow a valid format, and not attempt to spend funds that were already spent — this is what prevents "double-spending."
networks.validation.agreement.title: Agreement
networks.validation.agreement.text: A sufficient number of independent nodes — following the network's specific consensus mechanism — must independently reach the same conclusion about which transactions are valid and in what order, without trusting each other's word for it.
networks.validation.closing: The exact mechanism that produces this agreement — who is allowed to propose the next block, and how disagreements are resolved — is what's usually called the network's "consensus mechanism," a topic covered from the ground up in Module 01.
networks.gasFees.title: Gas Fees
networks.gasFees.p1: Producing and validating blocks costs real resources — computing power, storage, and bandwidth — and someone has to be compensated for providing them. "Gas" is the unit networks use to measure how much computational work a given transaction requires; the "gas fee" is the price paid, in the network's native token, for that work.
networks.gasFees.p2: Fees serve a second purpose beyond payment: they make it costly to spam the network with pointless transactions. Without any fee at all, flooding a network with millions of trivial transactions would be free, and blocks would fill up with noise instead of genuine activity.
networks.gasFees.field1.label: Gas Limit
networks.gasFees.field2.label: Gas Price
networks.gasFees.field3.label: Estimated Fee
networks.gasFees.p3: Fees rise and fall with how busy a network is at any given moment — more people trying to transact at once means more competition for the same block space, and higher fees to be included sooner. This lesson only covers what a fee represents, not how to speculate on its future cost.
networks.layer1.title: Layer 1
networks.layer1.p1: A Layer 1 is a base network — it has its own independent ledger, its own validators, and its own consensus mechanism. It doesn't depend on any other network to record or secure its transactions; it is the foundation.
networks.layer1.p2: Being the foundation comes with a trade-off: because every validator has to process every transaction directly, a Layer 1 that also wants a large, geographically spread set of independent validators usually can't also process an unlimited number of transactions per second. That trade-off is explored further in the next section.
networks.layer1.li1: Provides the ultimate security guarantee — everything else that builds on top of it inherits that security.
networks.layer1.li2: Changes to its core rules are slow and deliberate on purpose, since they affect everyone using it.
networks.layer1.li3: Transaction capacity is limited by how much every single validator can process.
networks.layer2.title: Layer 2
networks.layer2.p1: A Layer 2 is a separate system built on top of a Layer 1 to handle transactions faster and more cheaply, while still relying on that Layer 1 to guarantee security. Rather than every transaction being processed and stored directly by the base network, a Layer 2 processes many transactions itself and periodically settles a compressed summary back down to the Layer 1.
networks.layer2.p2: This means a Layer 2 inherits most of the security of its underlying Layer 1 without inheriting its capacity limits — at the cost of some added complexity, and, in some designs, a short delay before a transaction is considered fully final.
networks.layer2.l1.title: Layer 1
networks.layer2.l1.text: Processes and finalizes every transaction directly. Maximizes security and decentralization; capacity is limited.
networks.layer2.l2.title: Layer 2
networks.layer2.l2.text: Processes transactions off the base network and periodically settles a summary back to it. Higher throughput and lower fees, with inherited security.
networks.layer2.closing: Neither replaces the other — a healthy ecosystem generally uses a Layer 1 as the trust anchor and one or more Layer 2s to handle everyday volume cheaply.
networks.evmCompatible.title: EVM-Compatible Networks
networks.evmCompatible.p1: The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) is the standardized environment that executes smart contracts on Ethereum: a common set of instructions that every compliant node understands and runs identically. "EVM-compatible" simply means a different network chose to implement that same execution environment.
networks.evmCompatible.p2: The practical benefit is portability: a smart contract, developer tool, or wallet built for one EVM-compatible network usually works on another EVM-compatible network with little or no modification, because they're speaking the same underlying language.
networks.evmCompatible.li1: Developers can reuse code and tools across multiple networks instead of learning a new environment for each one.
networks.evmCompatible.li2: Wallets that support one EVM-compatible network can typically support others by simply adding a new network configuration.
networks.evmCompatible.li3: EVM compatibility describes the execution environment only — it says nothing about a network's validators, consensus mechanism, or security assumptions, which can still differ significantly.
networks.evmCompatible.li4: The same wallet address format is shared across EVM-compatible networks, which is convenient — but, as covered in the mistakes section, is also a common source of confusion.
networks.bridges.title: Bridges
networks.bridges.p1: Because every network keeps its own independent ledger, an asset that exists on one network doesn't natively exist on another — there is no built-in way for Network A to know anything happened on Network B. A bridge is the infrastructure that lets a user move value or information from one network to another despite this separation.
networks.bridges.p2: Most bridges follow one of two general patterns: locking an asset on the original network while minting a representative version on the destination network, or burning an asset on one side while releasing an equivalent that was already reserved on the other. In both cases, the user ends up with something on the destination network that represents value from the source network.
networks.bridges.li1: A bridge adds a new piece of infrastructure. It introduces a new point of trust between two networks that were not designed to talk to each other.
networks.bridges.li2: Bridges are a frequent target of large-scale exploits. They concentrate a lot of value in one place, which makes them attractive targets.
networks.bridges.li3: A bridged asset is a representation, not the original moved physically. Its value depends on the bridge continuing to honor that representation correctly.
networks.choosingNetwork.title: How to Choose a Network Correctly
networks.choosingNetwork.intro: There is no single "best" network — there is only the network that fits what you're actually trying to do. A short checklist helps make that decision deliberately instead of by default.
networks.choosingNetwork.li1: Purpose. What are you actually using the network for? Occasional transfers, running an application, or something else can point toward different priorities.
networks.choosingNetwork.li2: Fees and speed. How much does a typical transaction cost on this network, and how long does it take to be confirmed?
networks.choosingNetwork.li3: Security assumptions. How many independent validators does the network have, and how long has it operated without a major incident?
networks.choosingNetwork.li4: Ecosystem and tooling. Are the wallets, explorers, and applications you'll need actually well supported on this network?
networks.choosingNetwork.li5: Compatibility with what you already use. Does your existing wallet support this network natively, or would you need to add a new configuration?
networks.mistakes.title: Common Mistakes
networks.mistakes.intro: Nearly every network-related loss traces back to one of these avoidable mistakes.
networks.mistakes.li1: Sending an asset to the wrong network. The exact same address format can exist on multiple EVM-compatible networks — sending an asset intended for one network to that address on a different network can make it unrecoverable.
networks.mistakes.li2: Forgetting that fees are paid in the native token of that network. Having funds on a network is not the same as having enough of its native token to pay for a transaction.
networks.mistakes.li3: Assuming "EVM-compatible" means "identical." Compatible networks can still differ significantly in security assumptions, validator count, and maturity.
networks.mistakes.li4: Using a bridge without checking it first. Bridging funds through an unfamiliar or unaudited bridge concentrates risk in a single piece of infrastructure.
networks.mistakes.li5: Choosing a network based on hype instead of fit. The network that's trending is not automatically the one that matches your actual purpose, budget, or risk tolerance.
networks.summary.title: Summary
networks.summary.p1: A blockchain network is a set of nodes, a set of rules, and a way for those nodes to agree — validated transaction by validated transaction, block by block. Different networks make different trade-offs between decentralization, security, and speed, which is why so many exist side by side instead of one replacing all the others.
networks.summary.p2: Layer 1s provide the base security; Layer 2s scale on top of that security; EVM compatibility lets tools travel between networks; and bridges let value move between them — each with its own risks. Understanding these pieces is what lets you choose a network deliberately, instead of by accident.
networks.glossary.title: Glossary
networks.glossary.def1: A computer that keeps a copy of a network's ledger and enforces its rules.
networks.glossary.def2: The process by which independent nodes agree on which transactions are valid and in what order.
networks.glossary.def3: A participant responsible for checking and confirming transactions according to the network's consensus mechanism.
networks.glossary.def4: The unit used to measure the computational work required by a transaction.
networks.glossary.def5: The payment, in the network's native token, made to have a transaction processed.
networks.glossary.def6: A base blockchain network with its own independent ledger, validators, and consensus mechanism.
networks.glossary.def7: A system built on top of a Layer 1 that processes transactions off the base network and periodically settles a summary back to it.
networks.glossary.def8: The standardized execution environment for smart contracts that a network can choose to implement.
networks.glossary.def9: A network that implements the same execution environment as Ethereum, allowing tools and contracts to be reused across it.
networks.glossary.def10: Infrastructure that allows value or information to move between two otherwise separate networks.
networks.quiz.title: Quiz
networks.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
networks.quiz.q1: 1. What are the three ingredients that make up a blockchain network?
networks.quiz.q1o1: A. Nodes, a set of rules, and a consensus mechanism
networks.quiz.q1o3: C. A single central server and its administrator
networks.quiz.q1o4: D. A logo, a token symbol, and a website
networks.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review What Is a Blockchain Network and try again.
networks.quiz.q2: 2. Why do different blockchain networks exist?
networks.quiz.q2o1: A. Because only one network is technically allowed to exist
networks.quiz.q2o2: B. Because each network makes different trade-offs between decentralization, security, and speed
networks.quiz.q2o3: C. Because networks expire after a fixed number of transactions
networks.quiz.q2o4: D. Because every network must copy the exact design of the first one
networks.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Why Different Blockchains Exist and try again.
networks.quiz.q3: 3. What has to be true before a transaction is accepted by the network?
networks.quiz.q3o1: A. It only needs to be broadcast, nothing else
networks.quiz.q3o2: B. It must be correctly signed and independently agreed upon by enough nodes
networks.quiz.q3o3: C. It must be approved by a single central authority
networks.quiz.q3o4: D. It must be paid for in a currency outside the network
networks.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Transaction Validation and try again.
networks.quiz.q4: 4. What does a gas fee primarily pay for?
networks.quiz.q4o1: A. Marketing costs for the network
networks.quiz.q4o2: B. The computational work needed to process and validate a transaction
networks.quiz.q4o3: C. A monthly subscription to use the network
networks.quiz.q4o4: D. Insurance against losing your private key
networks.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Gas Fees and try again.
networks.quiz.q5: 5. What is the main difference between a Layer 1 and a Layer 2?
networks.quiz.q5o1: A. A Layer 2 replaces the Layer 1 entirely
networks.quiz.q5o2: B. A Layer 1 is the base network; a Layer 2 builds on top of it to scale while relying on its security
networks.quiz.q5o3: C. A Layer 1 only exists for testing
networks.quiz.q5o4: D. There is no meaningful difference between them
networks.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Layer 2 and try again.
networks.quiz.q6: 6. Why should you double-check the network before sending an asset to an address?
networks.quiz.q6o1: A. Because the same address format can exist on multiple networks, and sending to the wrong one can make funds unrecoverable
networks.quiz.q6o2: B. Because addresses expire after 24 hours
networks.quiz.q6o3: C. Because every network requires a different alphabet for addresses
networks.quiz.q6o4: D. Because it's only a cosmetic issue with no real consequence
networks.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Common Mistakes and try again.
assets.meta.title: Digital Assets — Nodra Academy
assets.hero.title: Digital Assets
assets.hero.description: The main types of assets in a blockchain ecosystem: coins, tokens, standards, supply, and how to tell a legitimate asset from a fake one.
assets.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
assets.hero.stat2.label: Duration
assets.hero.stat3.label: Format
assets.hero.stat4.label: Sections
assets.toc.title: Contents
assets.introduction.title: Introduction
assets.introduction.p1: Not everything that moves through a wallet is the same kind of thing. A blockchain ecosystem hosts many different digital assets, each built for a different purpose — some secure an entire network, others exist only inside a single application.
assets.introduction.p2: This lesson looks at what actually makes an asset a "coin" or a "token," how the main categories differ, how token standards let wallets and apps understand any compliant asset, and how supply and circulation work. None of this involves guessing at prices or making an investment decision — it's about recognizing what you're actually holding.
assets.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you will be able to explain the difference between a coin and a token, name the main categories of assets, recognize the most common token standards, and avoid the mistakes that most often lead beginners to lose funds to fake assets.
assets.coins.title: Coins
assets.coins.p1: A coin is the native asset of a blockchain network — it isn't built on top of anything else. It's the asset used to pay gas fees on that network, and, depending on the consensus mechanism, the asset staked or mined to help secure it.
assets.coins.p2: Because a coin is tied directly to its own network, it has no separate contract to inspect — it exists as part of the protocol's core rules, tracked directly by every node.
assets.coins.grid.title: Examples of Native Coins
assets.coins.ex1.title: Bitcoin (BTC)
assets.coins.ex1.text: The native coin of the Bitcoin network.
assets.coins.ex2.title: Ether (ETH)
assets.coins.ex2.text: The native coin of the Ethereum network, also used to pay for computation.
assets.coins.ex3.title: SOL
assets.coins.ex3.text: The native coin of the Solana network.
assets.coins.ex4.title: BNB
assets.coins.ex4.text: The native coin of the BNB Chain network.
assets.coins.ex5.title: AVAX
assets.coins.ex5.text: The native coin of the Avalanche network.
assets.tokens.title: Tokens
assets.tokens.p1: A token is an asset created through a smart contract deployed on top of an existing network — it doesn't have its own ledger, validators, or consensus mechanism. It relies entirely on the host network to record and secure its transactions.
assets.tokens.p2: Ethereum is a common example: thousands of tokens exist as smart contracts running on Ethereum, each following a technical standard so wallets and applications know how to read balances and process transfers.
assets.tokens.coin.title: Coin
assets.tokens.coin.text: Native to its own network. No separate contract. Used to pay that network's own gas fees.
assets.tokens.token.title: Token
assets.tokens.token.text: Created by a smart contract on top of an existing network. Relies on that network's security and consensus.
assets.tokens.closing: Every token has a host network — knowing which one is part of correctly identifying the asset, as covered later in this lesson.
assets.categories.title: Main Categories
assets.categories.intro: Tokens are commonly grouped by what they're designed to do. These categories aren't mutually exclusive, but they cover most of what you'll encounter.
assets.categories.utility.title: Utility Tokens
assets.categories.utility.text: Grant access to a specific application or service — for example, paying for computation, storage, or a feature within a platform.
assets.categories.governance.title: Governance Tokens
assets.categories.governance.text: Grant holders voting rights on proposals that affect how a protocol operates.
assets.categories.stablecoins.title: Stablecoins
assets.categories.stablecoins.text: Designed to track the value of a reference asset, such as a fiat currency, aiming for lower volatility relative to other digital assets.
assets.categories.wrapped.title: Wrapped Tokens
assets.categories.wrapped.text: A representation of an asset from one network, made usable on a different network — commonly created through a bridge.
assets.categories.nft.title: NFTs (Introduction)
assets.categories.nft.text: Non-fungible tokens: each one is unique and not interchangeable, commonly used to represent ownership of a specific, distinct item.
assets.tokenStandards.title: Token Standards
assets.tokenStandards.p1: A token standard is an agreed technical specification that any compliant contract follows, so wallets, exchanges, and marketplaces can interact with it without needing custom code for every single asset.
assets.tokenStandards.erc20.title: ERC-20
assets.tokenStandards.erc20.text: The standard for fungible tokens on Ethereum — every unit is identical and interchangeable, like a currency.
assets.tokenStandards.erc721.title: ERC-721
assets.tokenStandards.erc721.text: The standard for non-fungible tokens (NFTs) — each token is unique and not interchangeable with another.
assets.tokenStandards.erc1155.title: ERC-1155
assets.tokenStandards.erc1155.text: A multi-token standard that can represent both fungible and non-fungible items within a single contract.
assets.tokenStandards.closing: Other EVM-compatible networks generally reuse these same standards, while some non-EVM networks define their own equivalents — the underlying ideas of "fungible" and "non-fungible" carry over either way.
assets.supply.title: Supply
assets.supply.p1: "Circulating supply" refers to the units of an asset that currently exist and are accessible, while "max supply" refers to the upper limit a protocol's rules allow to ever exist, if one is defined at all.
assets.supply.p2: New units can be created over time through "emission," following rules set by the protocol. Units can also be permanently removed from circulation through "burning" — sending them to an address no one can access or spend from.
assets.supply.field1.label: Circulating Supply
assets.supply.field2.label: Max Supply
assets.supply.field3.label: Emission
assets.supply.field4.label: Burn
assets.supply.p3: Not every asset defines a max supply, and not every asset uses burning — these are design choices, not requirements.
assets.identifyingAssets.title: How to Identify an Asset Correctly
assets.identifyingAssets.p1: A name alone is not enough to identify an asset safely. Because anyone can deploy a token with any name or symbol they choose, correctly identifying an asset means checking several fields together.
assets.identifyingAssets.field1.label: Name
assets.identifyingAssets.field2.label: Ticker / Symbol
assets.identifyingAssets.field3.label: Contract Address
assets.identifyingAssets.field4.label: Network
assets.identifyingAssets.p2: A blockchain explorer lets you look up a contract address independently and confirm details like its creation date and current holders — a useful way to cross-check a project's own published information.
assets.identifyingAssets.closing: Copying only the name or ticker of an asset is risky: multiple contracts, on the same network or different ones, can share the exact same name.
assets.fakeTokens.title: Fake Tokens
assets.fakeTokens.intro: Because deploying a token contract requires no permission from anyone, imitations of well-known assets are a common and recurring problem.
assets.fakeTokens.li1: Cloned contracts. A new contract copies the name, symbol, and even the logo of a legitimate asset, but is a completely different, unrelated contract.
assets.fakeTokens.li2: Manufactured appearances of legitimacy. Some fake tokens are set up to look active or established, which alone doesn't confirm they're genuine.
assets.fakeTokens.li3: Always verify through official channels. Use the project's own published documentation or website, not search engine results or ads, to confirm the correct contract address.
assets.fakeTokens.li4: Explorers help, but aren't proof by themselves. Details like contract creation date or holder distribution are useful signals, but should be combined with official verification.
assets.useCases.title: Use Cases
assets.useCases.intro: Different categories of assets are typically used in different, concrete ways.
assets.useCases.li1: Payment. Sending a coin or token as payment for a service or transfer of value.
assets.useCases.li2: Governance. Using a governance token to vote on a proposal that changes how a protocol operates.
assets.useCases.li3: Access. Holding a utility token to unlock a specific feature or service within an application.
assets.useCases.li4: NFTs. Representing ownership of a single, distinct item that cannot be exchanged one-for-one with another.
assets.useCases.li5: Stablecoins. Using an asset designed for lower volatility for everyday transactions.
assets.mistakes.title: Common Mistakes
assets.mistakes.intro: Most losses involving digital assets trace back to one of these avoidable mistakes.
assets.mistakes.li1: Assuming a name guarantees legitimacy. Anyone can deploy a contract using any name or symbol.
assets.mistakes.li2: Sending an asset to a network that doesn't support it. Not every asset exists on every network, even when address formats look compatible.
assets.mistakes.li3: Confusing a wrapped asset with the original. A wrapped token is a different contract representing the original, not the original itself.
assets.mistakes.li4: Ignoring the difference between circulating and max supply. Comparing these numbers without understanding what each represents can be misleading.
assets.mistakes.li5: Trusting unofficial links or ads instead of official sources. The contract address should be confirmed through the project's own official channels.
assets.summary.title: Summary
assets.summary.p1: A coin is native to its own network; a token is created by a smart contract on top of an existing network. Assets are commonly grouped into categories like utility, governance, stablecoins, wrapped tokens, and NFTs, and follow technical standards so wallets and applications can handle them consistently.
assets.summary.p2: Supply concepts like circulation, emission, and burning describe how an asset's units change over time. Correctly identifying an asset means checking its name, ticker, contract address, and network together — never the name alone — which is also the best defense against fake tokens.
assets.glossary.title: Glossary
assets.glossary.def1: The native asset of a blockchain network, used to pay its gas fees.
assets.glossary.def2: An asset created by a smart contract on top of an existing network.
assets.glossary.def3: Self-executing code deployed on a network that defines how a token behaves.
assets.glossary.def4: A token that grants access to a specific application or service.
assets.glossary.def5: A token that grants holders voting rights over a protocol's decisions.
assets.glossary.def6: An asset designed to track the value of a reference asset, aiming for lower volatility.
assets.glossary.def7: A representation of an asset from one network, made usable on a different network.
assets.glossary.def8: A non-fungible token — unique and not interchangeable with another.
assets.glossary.def9: A shared technical specification that lets wallets and applications handle any compliant asset consistently.
assets.glossary.def10: Permanently removing units of an asset from circulation.
assets.quiz.title: Quiz
assets.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
assets.quiz.q1: 1. What defines a coin, as opposed to a token?
assets.quiz.q1o1: A. It is native to its own network and isn't created by a smart contract
assets.quiz.q1o2: B. It always has a higher supply than a token
assets.quiz.q1o3: C. It can only be used for payments
assets.quiz.q1o4: D. It is always created on Ethereum
assets.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Coins and try again.
assets.quiz.q2o1: A. A separate blockchain with its own validators
assets.quiz.q2o2: B. An asset created by a smart contract on top of an existing network
assets.quiz.q2o3: C. A physical representation of a coin
assets.quiz.q2o4: D. A backup copy of a coin
assets.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Tokens and try again.
assets.quiz.q3: 3. What is a governance token used for?
assets.quiz.q3o1: A. Paying gas fees on any network
assets.quiz.q3o2: B. Voting on proposals that affect how a protocol operates
assets.quiz.q3o3: C. Representing ownership of a unique item
assets.quiz.q3o4: D. Tracking the value of a fiat currency
assets.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Main Categories and try again.
assets.quiz.q4: 4. What is the key difference between ERC-20 and ERC-721?
assets.quiz.q4o1: A. ERC-20 is for fungible tokens; ERC-721 is for non-fungible tokens
assets.quiz.q4o2: B. ERC-721 is older than ERC-20
assets.quiz.q4o3: C. ERC-20 only works with NFTs
assets.quiz.q4o4: D. There is no meaningful difference between them
assets.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Token Standards and try again.
assets.quiz.q5o2: B. Permanently removing units from circulation
assets.quiz.q5o3: C. Converting it into a wrapped token
assets.quiz.q5o4: D. Moving it to a different network
assets.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Supply and try again.
assets.quiz.q6: 6. Why is copying only an asset's name risky?
assets.quiz.q6o1: A. Names are case-sensitive on every network
assets.quiz.q6o2: B. Multiple different contracts can share the exact same name
assets.quiz.q6o3: C. Names automatically expire after some time
assets.quiz.q6o4: D. It isn't risky at all
assets.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Identifying an Asset and try again.
assets.quiz.q7: 7. What is a common pattern behind fake tokens?
assets.quiz.q7o1: A. A cloned contract copying the name and symbol of a legitimate asset
assets.quiz.q7o2: B. Every token with a short name is fake
assets.quiz.q7o3: C. Fake tokens can only exist on test networks
assets.quiz.q7o4: D. Explorers automatically block fake tokens
assets.quiz.q7.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Fake Tokens and try again.
assets.quiz.q8: 8. What best describes a wrapped token?
assets.quiz.q8o1: A. The original asset moved physically to a new network
assets.quiz.q8o2: B. A representation of an asset from one network made usable on a different network
assets.quiz.q8o3: C. A token with no underlying asset at all
assets.quiz.q8o4: D. A governance token with extra voting power
assets.quiz.q8.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Main Categories and try again.
assets.quiz.q9: 9. What should you check before trusting a token's contract address?
assets.quiz.q9o1: A. Whether it appears in a search engine ad
assets.quiz.q9o2: B. The project's own official documentation or channels
assets.quiz.q9o3: C. Whether the name sounds trustworthy
assets.quiz.q9o4: D. Whether the ticker is short
assets.quiz.q9.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Common Mistakes and try again.

academyAbout.description: Every module is written to be understood, not skimmed — real explanations, real examples, and a quiz at the end to prove it stuck.
academyAbout.title: Teaching, Not Selling.
academyHero.description: Free, structured, hands-on courses — from your first block to advanced security practice. No hype, no shortcuts, just real understanding.
academyHero.title:

The Right

ecosystem.about.description: The people, principles and roadmap behind Nodra.
ecosystem.about.title: About
ecosystem.academy.description: Free, hands-on courses covering blockchain, wallets, DeFi and security — from first principles to advanced practice.
ecosystem.academy.title: Academy
ecosystem.community.description: A space for builders, learners and contributors to discuss ideas, ask questions and grow together.
ecosystem.community.title: Community
ecosystem.description: Nodra is not a single app — it's a growing set of independent products that share one mission: put understanding before speculation.
ecosystem.games.description: Learning through play — interactive simulations that make abstract concepts tangible.
ecosystem.games.title: Games
ecosystem.labs.description: Experimental tools and research projects exploring what's next in decentralized technology.
ecosystem.labs.title: Labs
ecosystem.title: One Brand, Several Products
ecosystem.tools.description: Practical utilities for builders and users — calculators, explorers and everyday crypto tooling.
ecosystem.tools.title: Tools

homeDashboard.footer: New verticals launch as they're ready
module.defi.description: What decentralized finance actually replaces, and how liquidity moves without a bank.
module.defi.title: DeFi Fundamentals
module.finalProject.description: Bring every track together in one applied capstone project, reviewed step by step.
module.finalProject.title: Final Project
module.lending.description: Collateral, interest rates, liquidations, and how on-chain lending markets stay solvent.
module.lending.title: Lending
module.security.description: Phishing, exploits, audits, and the habits that keep builders and users safe.
module.security.title: Security
module.stablecoins.description: Fiat-backed, crypto-backed and algorithmic stablecoins — and why some of them fail.
module.stablecoins.title: Stablecoins
module.yield.description: Liquidity pools, staking, farming, and telling real yield from unsustainable incentives.
module.yield.title: Yield
moduleAssets.glossary.def1: A shared technical specification (like ERC-20) that lets wallets and apps handle a token predictably.
moduleAssets.glossary.def2: A non-fungible token — a unique, non-interchangeable digital asset recorded on-chain.
moduleAssets.glossary.def3: A real-world asset represented on a blockchain, such as tokenized real estate or bonds.
moduleAssets.glossary.def4: The property of being interchangeable — one unit is identical in value to any other.
moduleAssets.glossary.def5: The act of creating a new unit of a token or NFT on-chain.
moduleAssets.hero.description: Not everything on a blockchain is the same kind of asset. This module explains how to tell them apart.
moduleAssets.hero.title: Assets
moduleAssets.learn.li1: The difference between a native coin and a token built on top of a network.
moduleAssets.learn.li2: What token standards like ERC-20 and ERC-721 actually define.
moduleAssets.learn.li3: Fungible vs. non-fungible assets, and why NFTs work differently from coins.
moduleAssets.learn.li4: How stablecoins attempt to hold a steady value.
moduleAssets.learn.li5: What a "real-world asset" (RWA) is when it's represented on-chain.
moduleAssets.meta.title: Assets — Nodra Academy
moduleAssets.overview.p1: "Crypto" is not one thing. A native coin like ETH, a token built on top of it, a stablecoin, and an NFT are all fundamentally different kinds of on-chain assets, with different rules for how they're created and transferred.
moduleAssets.overview.p2: This module will teach you to read a token's standard, tell fungible from non-fungible assets, understand what backs a stablecoin, and see how real-world assets (like real estate or bonds) get represented on-chain.
moduleAssets.overview.title: Assets
moduleDefi.glossary.def1: Automated Market Maker — a protocol that prices and swaps assets algorithmically, without an order book.
moduleDefi.glossary.def2: A pool of two or more assets locked in a smart contract, used to enable trading.
moduleDefi.glossary.def3: The difference between a trade's expected price and its actual executed price.
moduleDefi.glossary.def4: Decentralized Exchange — a trading protocol that runs without a central operator.
moduleDefi.glossary.def5: The temporary loss liquidity providers can face when pooled asset prices diverge.
moduleDefi.hero.description: DeFi rebuilds financial services as open protocols instead of institutions. This module explains how that works.
moduleDefi.hero.title: DeFi Fundamentals
moduleDefi.learn.li1: What problem DeFi actually solves compared to a traditional exchange.
moduleDefi.learn.li2: How an automated market maker (AMM) prices assets without an order book.
moduleDefi.learn.li3: What a liquidity pool is, and how liquidity providers earn fees.
moduleDefi.learn.li4: What impermanent loss is, and why it's a real cost, not just a risk.
moduleDefi.learn.li5: How to evaluate a DeFi protocol before using it.
moduleDefi.meta.title: DeFi Fundamentals — Nodra Academy
moduleDefi.overview.p1: Decentralized finance (DeFi) rebuilds services banks and exchanges normally provide — trading, lending, earning interest — as open protocols that anyone can use or build on, without a company custodying the funds.
moduleDefi.overview.p2: This module will teach you the core primitive behind almost every DeFi protocol: the automated market maker, how liquidity pools replace order books, and the real risks — like impermanent loss — that come with providing liquidity.
moduleDefi.overview.title: DeFi Fundamentals
moduleFinalProject.glossary.def1: A final project designed to apply everything learned across a full course track.
moduleFinalProject.glossary.def2: A collection of completed projects used to demonstrate practical skill.
moduleFinalProject.glossary.def3: A process where fellow learners evaluate and give feedback on submitted work.
moduleFinalProject.glossary.def4: The act of publishing a smart contract or application to a live network.
moduleFinalProject.glossary.def5: A blockchain network used for testing, where assets have no real value.
moduleFinalProject.hero.description: The capstone applies everything from Foundations, DeFi and Security in one connected project.
moduleFinalProject.hero.title: Final Project
moduleFinalProject.learn.li1: How the capstone project is structured, module by module.
moduleFinalProject.learn.li2: What deliverables will be expected by the end of the project.
moduleFinalProject.learn.li3: How the project will be evaluated, and against what criteria.
moduleFinalProject.learn.li4: How to set up a testnet environment to complete the project safely.
moduleFinalProject.learn.li5: How peer review will work once the full module is published.
moduleFinalProject.meta.title: Final Project — Nodra Academy
moduleFinalProject.overview.p1: The Final Project is the Academy's capstone: instead of a new topic, it asks you to apply what Modules 01 through 09 covered — blockchain fundamentals, wallets, DeFi mechanics, and security — to one connected, realistic scenario.
moduleFinalProject.overview.p2: This module will outline the project's structure, what will be evaluated, and how to submit your work for community review once the full module is published.
moduleFinalProject.overview.title: Final Project
moduleFinalProject.prereq.li3: Familiarity with the DeFi track (Modules 05–08) is recommended.
moduleLending.glossary.def1: An asset locked up to secure a loan, which can be seized if the loan isn't repaid.
moduleLending.glossary.def2: Loan-to-Value — the ratio between what you borrow and the value of your collateral.
moduleLending.glossary.def3: The forced sale of collateral when a loan's safety margin falls too low.
moduleLending.glossary.def4: The formula a lending protocol uses to set borrowing and supply rates.
moduleLending.glossary.def5: A live score representing how close a borrowing position is to liquidation.
moduleLending.hero.description: On-chain lending replaces a bank's credit check with collateral. This module explains how that trade-off works.
moduleLending.hero.title: Lending
moduleLending.learn.li1: Why on-chain lending needs over-collateralization instead of a credit score.
moduleLending.learn.li2: How interest rate models respond to supply and demand in real time.
moduleLending.learn.li3: What loan-to-value (LTV) means and why it determines your safety margin.
moduleLending.learn.li4: What actually happens during a liquidation, and who profits from it.
moduleLending.learn.li5: How to monitor a borrowing position's health factor.
moduleLending.meta.title: Lending — Nodra Academy
moduleLending.overview.p1: On-chain lending protocols let anyone borrow or lend assets without a credit check — because borrowers post collateral worth more than what they borrow, and that collateral can be automatically liquidated if its value falls.
moduleLending.overview.p2: This module will teach you how collateralization ratios work, how interest rates adjust with supply and demand, and exactly what happens — step by step — when a position gets liquidated.
moduleLending.overview.title: Lending
moduleNetworks.glossary.def1: A base blockchain network — like Ethereum or Solana — that handles its own consensus and security directly.
moduleNetworks.glossary.def2: The endpoint a wallet or app uses to read from and send transactions to a blockchain network.
moduleNetworks.glossary.def3: The fee paid to the network for processing a transaction or executing a smart contract.
moduleNetworks.glossary.def4: A protocol that moves assets or data from one blockchain network to another.
moduleNetworks.glossary.def5: The point at which a transaction is considered permanently and irreversibly confirmed.
moduleNetworks.hero.description: Blockchain isn't one network — it's many, each with different trade-offs. This module explains how to reason about them.
moduleNetworks.hero.title: Networks
moduleNetworks.learn.li1: How Ethereum, Solana, and BNB Chain differ in design and trade-offs.
moduleNetworks.learn.li2: What gas fees are actually paying for, and why they change.
moduleNetworks.learn.li3: How cross-chain bridges move assets between networks — and their risks.
moduleNetworks.learn.li4: What "finality" means and why it matters before you consider a transaction safe.
moduleNetworks.learn.li5: How to choose the right network for a given use case.
moduleNetworks.meta.title: Networks — Nodra Academy
moduleNetworks.overview.p1: Bitcoin proved one blockchain could work. Since then, hundreds of networks have launched, each making different trade-offs between speed, cost, and decentralization — Ethereum, Solana, and BNB Chain among the most used.
moduleNetworks.overview.p2: This module will teach you how to compare networks on their own terms: what a Layer 1 actually is, how transactions get confirmed, what gas fees pay for, and how assets move from one chain to another through bridges.
moduleNetworks.overview.title: Networks
modulePlaceholder.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
modulePlaceholder.hero.stat2.label: Duration
modulePlaceholder.hero.stat3.label: Track
modulePlaceholder.hero.stat4.label: Sections
moduleSecurity.glossary.def1: An independent review of a smart contract's code, looking for bugs and vulnerabilities.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def2: An attack that takes advantage of a specific bug or design flaw to steal or misdirect funds.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def3: A scam where a project's creators withdraw all liquidity or funds, abandoning users.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def4: A common smart contract bug where a function is re-entered before its state is updated.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def5: Operational security — the personal habits that reduce your exposure to attacks.
moduleSecurity.hero.description: Most losses in this space are preventable. This module explains the habits and red flags that matter most.
moduleSecurity.hero.title: Security
moduleSecurity.learn.li1: The most common phishing patterns used against wallet holders.
moduleSecurity.learn.li2: What kinds of bugs smart contract exploits typically take advantage of.
moduleSecurity.learn.li3: What an audit actually verifies — and its real limitations.
moduleSecurity.learn.li4: How rug pulls are structured, and the warning signs that precede them.
moduleSecurity.learn.li5: The personal security habits (opsec) that remove most realistic risk.
moduleSecurity.meta.title: Security — Nodra Academy
moduleSecurity.overview.p1: The overwhelming majority of losses in crypto come from a small set of repeated patterns: phishing, contract exploits, and avoidable operational mistakes — not exotic, unstoppable attacks.
moduleSecurity.overview.p2: This module will teach you to recognize phishing attempts, understand what a smart contract audit does and doesn't guarantee, and build the personal security habits that remove most realistic risk.
moduleSecurity.overview.title: Security
moduleSecurity.prereq.li2: Familiarity with the DeFi track (Modules 05–08) is recommended.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def1: The target value a stablecoin is designed to maintain, usually a fiat currency.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def2: The value of backing assets held relative to the stablecoins issued.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def3: An event where a stablecoin's market price moves meaningfully away from its target peg.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def4: A report (not a full audit) confirming a stablecoin issuer's reserves at a point in time.
moduleStablecoins.glossary.def5: A stablecoin that uses code and incentives, rather than collateral, to hold its peg.
moduleStablecoins.hero.description: Stablecoins try to hold a steady value in a volatile market. This module explains the three ways they attempt it.
moduleStablecoins.hero.title: Stablecoins
moduleStablecoins.learn.li1: The three main stablecoin designs: fiat-backed, crypto-backed, and algorithmic.
moduleStablecoins.learn.li2: What a reserve attestation does — and doesn't — prove about a stablecoin.
moduleStablecoins.learn.li3: Why over-collateralization is used to absorb crypto price volatility.
moduleStablecoins.learn.li4: What a "depeg" is, and historical examples of why it happened.
moduleStablecoins.learn.li5: How to evaluate a stablecoin's real backing before relying on it.
moduleStablecoins.meta.title: Stablecoins — Nodra Academy
moduleStablecoins.overview.p1: A stablecoin is a token designed to hold a steady value, usually pegged to a currency like the US dollar. How that peg is maintained differs enormously between designs — and that difference is exactly where the risk lives.
moduleStablecoins.overview.p2: This module will teach you to tell fiat-backed, crypto-backed, and algorithmic stablecoins apart, understand what "reserve attestation" actually proves, and recognize the warning signs that precede a depeg.
moduleStablecoins.overview.title: Stablecoins
moduleYield.glossary.def1: Annual Percentage Yield — a return rate that accounts for compounding.
moduleYield.glossary.def2: Locking an asset to help secure a network (or protocol) in exchange for rewards.
moduleYield.glossary.def3: Moving capital between DeFi protocols to chase the highest available return.
moduleYield.glossary.def4: New tokens a protocol distributes as rewards, often to attract early liquidity.
moduleYield.glossary.def5: Total Value Locked — the total value of assets deposited in a protocol.
moduleYield.hero.description: Not all yield is created equal. This module explains how to tell a sustainable return from a temporary incentive.
moduleYield.hero.title: Yield
moduleYield.learn.li1: The difference between staking rewards, trading fees, and token emissions.
moduleYield.learn.li2: Why APY and APR are calculated differently, and why that matters.
moduleYield.learn.li3: How to read Total Value Locked (TVL) as a signal, not a guarantee.
moduleYield.learn.li4: Why very high advertised yields are usually a warning sign, not a bonus.
moduleYield.learn.li5: How to evaluate whether a yield source can realistically continue.
moduleYield.meta.title: Yield — Nodra Academy
moduleYield.overview.p1: "Yield" in DeFi can come from very different places: securing a network through staking, earning trading fees by providing liquidity, or simply being paid in a protocol's own token to attract early users.
moduleYield.overview.p2: This module will teach you to separate these sources, understand why advertised APYs can be misleading, and recognize the signs of yield that exists only to be unsustainable.
moduleYield.overview.title: Yield

tracks.defi.title: DeFi
tracks.foundations.description: Blocks, consensus, wallets, networks and asset types — the base every other track builds on.
tracks.foundations.title: Blockchain Foundations
tracks.security.description: Threat models, common exploits, and a final project that ties every track together.
tracks.security.title: Security
